REPLICABILITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper is largely reproducible: code is available, it uses public UD 1.2 test sections and standard baselines (delexicalized TurboParser), and evaluation is via UAS which is standard. However, key implementation details are missing or under-specified for full replication: hyperparameters and model/configuration settings are not reported, the preprocessing pipeline is not fully described, and several evaluation sets were author-annotated (Bible, Magazine, QuestionBank) without sufficient detail on annotation format or release. Given available code and clear methodological description of UD head rules and personalization/decoding, a reimplementation is feasible but would require non-trivial effort to fill these gaps. | B(3): The paper makes reproduction feasible in part: it is training-free and reports the datasets (UD 1.2 test sets, plus specific additional corpora) and evaluation metric (UAS), and the authors provide code. However, key reproducibility elements are missing or unclear: hyperparameters are not reported; the preprocessing pipeline and exact handling of UD head-rule application and personalization setup are not fully specified in the summary; several evaluation sets were author-annotated (Bible, Magazine, QuestionBank) with no indication these annotations are public. Given these omissions, a reimplementation from the paper alone would require nontrivial guesswork.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper is largely reproducible: code is available, it uses public UD 1.2 test sections and standard baselines (delexicalized TurboParser), and evaluation is via UAS which is standard. However, key implementation details are missing or under-specified for full replication: hyperparameters and model/configuration settings are not reported, the preprocessing pipeline is not fully described, and several evaluation sets were author-annotated (Bible, Magazine, QuestionBank) without sufficient detail on annotation format or release. Given available code and clear methodological description of UD head rules and personalization/decoding, a reimplementation is feasible but would require non-trivial effort to fill these gaps.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper makes reproduction feasible in part: it is training-free and reports the datasets (UD 1.2 test sets, plus specific additional corpora) and evaluation metric (UAS), and the authors provide code. However, key reproducibility elements are missing or unclear: hyperparameters are not reported; the preprocessing pipeline and exact handling of UD head-rule application and personalization setup are not fully specified in the summary; several evaluation sets were author-annotated (Bible, Magazine, QuestionBank) with no indication these annotations are public. Given these omissions, a reimplementation from the paper alone would require nontrivial guesswork.

IMPACT.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(3): The paper tackles an important practical problem—language-agnostic dependency parsing without training—and demonstrates a pragmatic, UD-compliant, rule-based system (UDP) that is competitive on 32 languages and robust to domain shifts, even outperforming multi-source delexicalized transfer in some settings. From an empirical-methodology standpoint this is a useful contribution because it shows that careful use of UD constraints, head rules, and personalized PageRank can yield strong zero-training baselines that are easy to reproduce (code provided). However, limitations—evaluation only in UAS, acknowledged failures on multiword expressions/coordination/proper names, and lack of hyperparameter reporting—reduce its potential to fundamentally change parsing research; it is more likely to spawn follow-up work and practical use-cases than to be transformative. | B(4): The paper tackles an important problem—zero-training dependency parsing for UD—which is highly relevant for low-resource languages and rapid deployment. Its training-free approach that combines UD head rules, personalized PageRank for predicate identification, and test-time estimation of adposition direction is practical and evaluated across 32 languages with competitive results versus multi-source delexicalized transfer, indicating potential to change practice for resource-scarce settings. While limitations (drop under the CF POS scenario, no handling of MWEs/coordination/names, and only UAS evaluation) constrain scope, the method’s robustness to domain change and available code make it likely to be adopted and extended, opening useful follow-up research directions.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper tackles an important practical problem—language-agnostic dependency parsing without training—and demonstrates a pragmatic, UD-compliant, rule-based system (UDP) that is competitive on 32 languages and robust to domain shifts, even outperforming multi-source delexicalized transfer in some settings. From an empirical-methodology standpoint this is a useful contribution because it shows that careful use of UD constraints, head rules, and personalized PageRank can yield strong zero-training baselines that are easy to reproduce (code provided). However, limitations—evaluation only in UAS, acknowledged failures on multiword expressions/coordination/proper names, and lack of hyperparameter reporting—reduce its potential to fundamentally change parsing research; it is more likely to spawn follow-up work and practical use-cases than to be transformative.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper tackles an important problem—zero-training dependency parsing for UD—which is highly relevant for low-resource languages and rapid deployment. Its training-free approach that combines UD head rules, personalized PageRank for predicate identification, and test-time estimation of adposition direction is practical and evaluated across 32 languages with competitive results versus multi-source delexicalized transfer, indicating potential to change practice for resource-scarce settings. While limitations (drop under the CF POS scenario, no handling of MWEs/coordination/names, and only UAS evaluation) constrain scope, the method’s robustness to domain change and available code make it likely to be adopted and extended, opening useful follow-up research directions.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper compares to sensible baselines (a rule-based head-rule baseline, adjacency baseline, and a multi-source delexicalized transfer parser) using UD test sections and UAS, and discusses when prior methods match or outperform UDP. However, a methodological concern reduces fairness: the paper estimates adposition direction directly on test data, which constitutes leakage and undermines strict comparability. While related work coverage is reported as thorough and the paper analyzes differences and limitations (e.g., CF POS scenario, domain robustness), the experimental setup has this notable caveat and may omit some recent unsupervised/parsing baselines, so overall the comparisons are good but not fully comprehensive or strictly fair. | B(4): The paper compares against reasonable, relevant baselines (a rule-based head-rule baseline, a multi-source delexicalized transfer parser, and simple adjacency baselines) and evaluates on UD test sections using UAS, so comparisons are largely fair. The authors also report where their method matches or outperforms the delexicalized transfer system and acknowledge limitations, and related work coverage is described as thorough. Minor omissions remain: I would have liked explicit comparison to other contemporary unsupervised or grammar-based UD-aware parsers and clearer hyperparameter/control reporting to fully rule out hidden advantages, but overall the experimental comparisons are solid and appropriately analyzed.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper compares to sensible baselines (a rule-based head-rule baseline, adjacency baseline, and a multi-source delexicalized transfer parser) using UD test sections and UAS, and discusses when prior methods match or outperform UDP. However, a methodological concern reduces fairness: the paper estimates adposition direction directly on test data, which constitutes leakage and undermines strict comparability. While related work coverage is reported as thorough and the paper analyzes differences and limitations (e.g., CF POS scenario, domain robustness), the experimental setup has this notable caveat and may omit some recent unsupervised/parsing baselines, so overall the comparisons are good but not fully comprehensive or strictly fair.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper compares against reasonable, relevant baselines (a rule-based head-rule baseline, a multi-source delexicalized transfer parser, and simple adjacency baselines) and evaluates on UD test sections using UAS, so comparisons are largely fair. The authors also report where their method matches or outperforms the delexicalized transfer system and acknowledge limitations, and related work coverage is described as thorough. Minor omissions remain: I would have liked explicit comparison to other contemporary unsupervised or grammar-based UD-aware parsers and clearer hyperparameter/control reporting to fully rule out hidden advantages, but overall the experimental comparisons are solid and appropriately analyzed.

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper appears well-organized with a clear logical flow from introduction through method to experiments and conclusion, and the main contributions are stated early (training-free UDP, use of personalized PageRank, and test-time adposition direction estimation). Notation and definitions are reported as consistent and the writing quality is described as good, making the core ideas easy to follow. One weakness for presentation is the lack of reported hyperparameters and some missing detail on figures/tables effectiveness in the summary, which may leave a few experimental details under-specified for readers. Overall it is well-written with only minor presentation shortcomings. | B(4): The paper is overall well organized with a clear flow from problem motivation to method to experiments and conclusion, and its main contributions are stated early (training-free UDP, use of personalized PageRank, and adposition-direction estimation). Notation appears consistent and definitions are provided for UD-aware components, and figures/tables/examples seem to support understanding of the algorithm and results. Writing quality is good with few grammatical issues, but presentation could be improved by reporting hyperparameters and clarifying some experimental details (e.g., handling of multiword expressions and edge labeling) to aid reproducibility.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper appears well-organized with a clear logical flow from introduction through method to experiments and conclusion, and the main contributions are stated early (training-free UDP, use of personalized PageRank, and test-time adposition direction estimation). Notation and definitions are reported as consistent and the writing quality is described as good, making the core ideas easy to follow. One weakness for presentation is the lack of reported hyperparameters and some missing detail on figures/tables effectiveness in the summary, which may leave a few experimental details under-specified for readers. Overall it is well-written with only minor presentation shortcomings.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper is overall well organized with a clear flow from problem motivation to method to experiments and conclusion, and its main contributions are stated early (training-free UDP, use of personalized PageRank, and adposition-direction estimation). Notation appears consistent and definitions are provided for UD-aware components, and figures/tables/examples seem to support understanding of the algorithm and results. Writing quality is good with few grammatical issues, but presentation could be improved by reporting hyperparameters and clarifying some experimental details (e.g., handling of multiword expressions and edge labeling) to aid reproducibility.

ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper presents a notable and uncommon combination — a completely training-free UD parser that leverages UD POS/head-rule constraints, personalized PageRank to surface main predicates, and on-the-fly estimation of adposition direction — which clearly differentiates it from standard trained or multi-source transfer systems. While the core ingredients (head rules, graph ranking) are known, assembling them into a principled, language-agnostic, no-training pipeline that performs competitively across 32 languages is a non-trivial and informative contribution that would surprise practitioners used to trained or transfer approaches. | B(4): The paper presents a notable and well-differentiated idea: a truly training-free UD parser that combines UD head-rule constraints with personalized PageRank to identify predicates and guide attachments, and it even estimates adposition direction directly on test data. While unsupervised and rule-based dependency parsing are well-studied, the specific combination of UD-aware constraints, graph-ranking for predicate identification, and test-time estimation of adposition direction constitutes a non-trivial methodological departure from standard delexicalized transfer or purely statistical unsupervised approaches. Given these design choices and the reported multilingual robustness, the contribution would inform experts in low-resource and UD-focused parsing, though it stops short of a radical conceptual breakthrough.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper presents a notable and uncommon combination — a completely training-free UD parser that leverages UD POS/head-rule constraints, personalized PageRank to surface main predicates, and on-the-fly estimation of adposition direction — which clearly differentiates it from standard trained or multi-source transfer systems. While the core ingredients (head rules, graph ranking) are known, assembling them into a principled, language-agnostic, no-training pipeline that performs competitively across 32 languages is a non-trivial and informative contribution that would surprise practitioners used to trained or transfer approaches.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper presents a notable and well-differentiated idea: a truly training-free UD parser that combines UD head-rule constraints with personalized PageRank to identify predicates and guide attachments, and it even estimates adposition direction directly on test data. While unsupervised and rule-based dependency parsing are well-studied, the specific combination of UD-aware constraints, graph-ranking for predicate identification, and test-time estimation of adposition direction constitutes a non-trivial methodological departure from standard delexicalized transfer or purely statistical unsupervised approaches. Given these design choices and the reported multilingual robustness, the contribution would inform experts in low-resource and UD-focused parsing, though it stops short of a radical conceptual breakthrough.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper addresses a core NLP problem (dependency parsing for Universal Dependencies) and proposes a novel, lightweight unsupervised method that is directly relevant to top-tier NLP/ML audiences interested in multilingual and zero-resource parsing. Evaluation uses UD test sets across 32 languages and compares against sensible baselines (delexicalized transfer, adjacency, rule baseline), and the code is available — all positive signals for venue fit. I note some empirical reproducibility gaps (no hyperparameter reporting, reliance on small author-annotated datasets for some claims, and only UAS evaluation) that are minor for scope but worth addressing for full acceptance. Overall the work is a good fit for a top conference, with primarily empirical detail omissions rather than a mismatch in topic or audience. | B(4): This paper is broadly within scope for a top NLP venue: it tackles unsupervised dependency parsing for Universal Dependencies, introduces a novel training-free parser that leverages UD head rules and personalized PageRank, and evaluates on 32 languages with comparisons to reasonable baselines (delexicalized multi-source transfer, adjacency, rule baseline). The formulation and evaluation (UAS, cross-lingual robustness, test-time estimation of adposition direction) match community expectations, and the method targets the UD/low-resource audience I represent. Remaining concerns for full top-tier fit are the restriction to unlabeled attachment, lack of handling MWEs/coordination/proper names, the CF POS failure mode, and some reporting gaps (e.g., hyperparameters and reliance on UD 1.2 test splits), which make this a strong but not perfect match.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper addresses a core NLP problem (dependency parsing for Universal Dependencies) and proposes a novel, lightweight unsupervised method that is directly relevant to top-tier NLP/ML audiences interested in multilingual and zero-resource parsing. Evaluation uses UD test sets across 32 languages and compares against sensible baselines (delexicalized transfer, adjacency, rule baseline), and the code is available — all positive signals for venue fit. I note some empirical reproducibility gaps (no hyperparameter reporting, reliance on small author-annotated datasets for some claims, and only UAS evaluation) that are minor for scope but worth addressing for full acceptance. Overall the work is a good fit for a top conference, with primarily empirical detail omissions rather than a mismatch in topic or audience.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper is broadly within scope for a top NLP venue: it tackles unsupervised dependency parsing for Universal Dependencies, introduces a novel training-free parser that leverages UD head rules and personalized PageRank, and evaluates on 32 languages with comparisons to reasonable baselines (delexicalized multi-source transfer, adjacency, rule baseline). The formulation and evaluation (UAS, cross-lingual robustness, test-time estimation of adposition direction) match community expectations, and the method targets the UD/low-resource audience I represent. Remaining concerns for full top-tier fit are the restriction to unlabeled attachment, lack of handling MWEs/coordination/proper names, the CF POS failure mode, and some reporting gaps (e.g., hyperparameters and reliance on UD 1.2 test splits), which make this a strong but not perfect match.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper's main claims are clearly stated and largely supported by experiments across 32 languages and comparisons to sensible baselines (rule-based head rules, multi-source delexicalized transfer, adjacency), and the method's design (UD head rules + PageRank + UD constraints) is plausibly evaluated. However, I see methodological gaps that prevent a flawless endorsement: no hyperparameter/runtime reporting (even if training-free), lack of statistical significance testing, and reliance on small, author-annotated test sets for some evaluations; estimating adposition direction directly on test data also raises concerns about test-set-informed choices. The authors acknowledge important limitations (CF POS scenario, MWEs/coordination, unlabeled-only evaluation), so conclusions are generally justified but tempered by these methodological omissions. | B(4): The paper's core claims are largely supported by experiments across 32 languages and by a sensible, UD-aware rule-based algorithm augmented with personalized PageRank; code availability increases reproducibility. However, there are technical omissions: no hyperparameter reporting, lack of statistical significance testing, and the methodological choice to estimate adposition direction directly on test data merits clearer justification as it could conflate development and evaluation. Mathematical content is lightweight and appears correct for a rule-based system; overall these are minor issues that do not undermine the main technical conclusions.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper's main claims are clearly stated and largely supported by experiments across 32 languages and comparisons to sensible baselines (rule-based head rules, multi-source delexicalized transfer, adjacency), and the method's design (UD head rules + PageRank + UD constraints) is plausibly evaluated. However, I see methodological gaps that prevent a flawless endorsement: no hyperparameter/runtime reporting (even if training-free), lack of statistical significance testing, and reliance on small, author-annotated test sets for some evaluations; estimating adposition direction directly on test data also raises concerns about test-set-informed choices. The authors acknowledge important limitations (CF POS scenario, MWEs/coordination, unlabeled-only evaluation), so conclusions are generally justified but tempered by these methodological omissions.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper's core claims are largely supported by experiments across 32 languages and by a sensible, UD-aware rule-based algorithm augmented with personalized PageRank; code availability increases reproducibility. However, there are technical omissions: no hyperparameter reporting, lack of statistical significance testing, and the methodological choice to estimate adposition direction directly on test data merits clearer justification as it could conflate development and evaluation. Mathematical content is lightweight and appears correct for a rule-based system; overall these are minor issues that do not undermine the main technical conclusions.

SUBSTANCE.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(3): The paper presents broad multilingual evaluations (32 languages) and domain-robustness tests and compares to reasonable baselines, which supports many of its performance claims, but important empirical analyses are missing. There is no mention of thorough ablation or sensitivity studies (e.g., impact of the personalized PageRank component or design choices), and hyperparameters are not reported despite code availability, hindering reproducibility. Related work appears well covered and several failure modes and limitations are explicitly discussed, but the lack of detailed experimental diagnostics and reporting reduces confidence in isolating why components succeed or fail. | B(4): The experiments are broad (32 languages, domain-robustness tests, and multiple baselines including multi-source delexicalized transfer) and the paper candidly reports some failure modes (e.g. CF POS scenario) and limitations (MWEs, coordination, labels). Related work coverage is reported as thorough and code is available, aiding reproducibility. However, the paper lacks explicit ablation studies/sensitivity analyses for key components (e.g. contribution of personalized PageRank vs. head-rule graph) and does not report hyperparameter choices or thorough stability checks (adposition-direction estimation on test data could also merit deeper analysis), preventing a top score.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper presents broad multilingual evaluations (32 languages) and domain-robustness tests and compares to reasonable baselines, which supports many of its performance claims, but important empirical analyses are missing. There is no mention of thorough ablation or sensitivity studies (e.g., impact of the personalized PageRank component or design choices), and hyperparameters are not reported despite code availability, hindering reproducibility. Related work appears well covered and several failure modes and limitations are explicitly discussed, but the lack of detailed experimental diagnostics and reporting reduces confidence in isolating why components succeed or fail.

reviewer_b.justification: The experiments are broad (32 languages, domain-robustness tests, and multiple baselines including multi-source delexicalized transfer) and the paper candidly reports some failure modes (e.g. CF POS scenario) and limitations (MWEs, coordination, labels). Related work coverage is reported as thorough and code is available, aiding reproducibility. However, the paper lacks explicit ablation studies/sensitivity analyses for key components (e.g. contribution of personalized PageRank vs. head-rule graph) and does not report hyperparameter choices or thorough stability checks (adposition-direction estimation on test data could also merit deeper analysis), preventing a top score.

main_claims: Presented UDP, an unsupervised dependency parser for Universal Dependencies that requires no training and leverages UD POS constraints and head rules.
Incorporated personalized PageRank to identify main predicates and improve attachment of content words.
Estimated adposition (pre/postposition) direction directly on test data, making the method free of language-specific parameters.
Demonstrated competitive performance on 32 languages and robustness to domain change, outperforming or matching a multi-source delexicalized transfer system on some settings.
_summary.methodology: A rule-based, training-free dependency parser that builds a graph from UD-compliant head rules, uses personalized PageRank to rank content words (with personalization favoring the estimated main predicate), and decodes dependencies while enforcing UD constraints (e.g., function words as leaves), estimating adposition direction on test data.
datasets: King James Bible (first 50 verses, annotated by authors)
Magazine dataset (50 sentences, annotated by authors)
baselines: Multi-source delexicalized transfer parser (MSD / delexicalized TurboParser)
limitations: Large drop in performance under the extreme content-vs-function (CF) POS scenario.
Does not handle multiword expressions, coordination, and proper names (noted as future work).
Only evaluated for unlabeled attachment; edge labeling not addressed.
_summary.topic_area: NLP - unsupervised dependency parsing / Universal Dependencies

reviewer_a.name: Empirical Methodologist
reviewer_a.background: Senior ML engineer and researcher with extensive experience evaluating parsing algorithms, reproducibility practices, and empirical rigor in NLP. Has led benchmark suites and contributed to replication studies in syntactic parsing and unsupervised learning.
traits: carefully checks experimental design, including baselines, hyperparameters, and train/test separation
values thorough ablation studies and sensitivity analyses to isolate component contributions
attentive to reproducibility: requests clear implementation details, datasets, seeds, and runtime/complexity reporting
insists on appropriate evaluation metrics, statistical significance testing, and fair comparisons to prior work
reviewer_a.reviewing_style: Focuses on the empirical validity and reproducibility of claims, ensuring that experimental evidence supports each major conclusion.

reviewer_b.name: UD & Syntax Specialist
reviewer_b.background: Senior computational linguist and long-term contributor to Universal Dependencies resources and evaluation tasks, with a track record of work on cross-lingual and unsupervised parsing methods and typological analysis.
traits: examines novelty and conceptual fit with prior UD-aware and unsupervised parsing approaches
evaluates linguistic plausibility, cross-linguistic coverage, and the method's reliance on linguistic resources or assumptions
values broader impact: usability for low-resource languages, contribution to UD ecosystem, and practical deployment concerns
notices alignment (or lack thereof) with typological variation and whether language-specific heuristics are truly avoided
reviewer_b.reviewing_style: Assesses the paper's novelty, linguistic soundness, and potential impact on the UD community and low-resource parsing research.
